title: WHO-style fever danger signs
text: Fever with difficulty breathing, chest pain, confusion, stiff neck, persistent vomiting, dehydration, rash that does not blanch, or fever lasting more than three days should be assessed by a clinician. Children, older adults, pregnant people, and immunocompromised patients need earlier medical review.

title: Viral fever overview
text: Viral fever commonly presents with fever, headache, body pain, fatigue, sore throat, cough, or runny nose. Supportive care includes rest, fluids, and temperature control. Warning signs include breathing difficulty, chest pain, confusion, severe weakness, dehydration, persistent high fever, or worsening symptoms.

title: Influenza clinical pattern
text: Influenza can cause abrupt fever, chills, dry cough, headache, muscle aches, fatigue, sore throat, and runny nose. Higher risk groups include older adults, young children, pregnant people, and people with chronic disease. Antiviral treatment works best when started early by a clinician.

title: Dengue warning signs
text: Dengue may present with high fever, severe headache, pain behind the eyes, joint and muscle pain, rash, nausea, vomiting, or bleeding. Warning signs include abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, fluid accumulation, mucosal bleeding, lethargy, restlessness, liver enlargement, or rising hematocrit with falling platelets.

title: Chest pain urgent symptoms
text: Chest pain with shortness of breath, sweating, fainting, pain radiating to arm or jaw, severe weakness, or known heart disease needs urgent medical evaluation. Do not delay emergency care for suspected heart attack or severe breathing difficulty.

title: Glucose and HbA1c interpretation
text: High fasting glucose or elevated HbA1c can suggest impaired glucose regulation or diabetes risk. Diagnosis needs repeat testing and clinician review. Very high glucose with vomiting, dehydration, confusion, or rapid breathing can be urgent.

title: CBC interpretation overview
text: Complete blood count interpretation depends on hemoglobin, white blood cell count, differential count, and platelets. Low hemoglobin can indicate anemia. High white blood cells may reflect infection or inflammation. Low platelets can increase bleeding risk and needs medical context.
